context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m stuck on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for Urban Studies 11.024x. Can u just help me fill it out for my city if I give u the city name?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you work through it, but I won’t fill it out for you. You need to build the spider diagram yourself.

Start with this: what city did you choose, and which option are you using—the table or the hand-drawn diagram?

Then tell me one thing you already know about that city’s climate challenge or climate action. From there, we’ll build it piece by piece.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and avoid completing the assignment for the student. Because the student is asking me to fill out the spider diagram, I should refuse to do the work for them and instead use guided discovery. I will keep the response brief, set boundaries, and ask a targeted question that moves directly toward completing the assignment: identifying the city and what the student already knows about its climate risks or actions. This supports the assignment without giving answers directly or writing the submission for the student.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, and I’m doing the table version. Tbh I’m short on time, so can u just give me a solid draft row-by-row and I’ll tweak wording after?
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be me doing the assignment for you.

We’ll do this efficiently instead. For Jakarta, start with one pathway only.
What is the clearest climate-related problem Jakarta faces: flooding, heat, air pollution, sea-level rise, land subsidence, or something else?

Pick one, and then tell me: who in the city is responding to it—government, communities, businesses, or infrastructure agencies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which I must refuse. I should keep the response concise, uphold boundaries, and use Socratic questioning that advances the task. The best next step is to have the student generate content for one row at a time, starting from a city-specific issue they likely already know, while I guide structure and relevance to the 5 Pathways without drafting the submission.

exchanges.student: probably flooding + land subsidence, and I guess gov agencies are responding. But like I need smth usable rn — can u at least phrase 2–3 concise points I could put in the table for that pathway?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write points for your table. You need to do that part.

But here’s the fastest path: for the flooding/subsidence pathway, make 3 bullets answering these:
1) What is the risk?
2) What action is Jakarta taking?
3) What limitation or tradeoff exists?

Try it in your own words—short fragments are fine. Give me your 3 rough bullets, and I’ll tell you if they fit the pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for phrasing they can directly place into the table, which would cross into writing the assignment for them. I should refuse to provide ready-to-submit wording, but still help efficiently by prompting them to generate the content in a structured way. I will narrow the task to one pathway and ask targeted questions that elicit 2–3 points from the student. This keeps the interaction bite-sized, uses guided discovery, and moves directly toward a usable draft without giving it.

exchanges.student:
ok rough try:  
- Risk: severe flooding gets worse bc parts of Jakarta are sinking and sea level rise adds pressure  
- Action: sea walls, drainage/pumps, and maybe relocating some stuff  
- Limitation: expensive, doesn’t fix root cause, and low-income areas still get hit  

Can u make these sound more academic/professional so I can paste them?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish or rewrite it for you. That’s your writing.

Your content is usable, though. To make it more academic, tighten each bullet with one concrete detail: which actor, which infrastructure/action, and which inequity or tradeoff.

Pick one bullet and revise it yourself with a specific example from Jakarta. Which one do you want to strengthen first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for polishing language they can paste, which the rules explicitly forbid. I should refuse to edit or write for them, while still supporting their learning. A concise response should acknowledge that their content is on the right track and redirect them to strengthen it themselves by making each bullet more specific with evidence, actors, and tradeoffs. I will ask one focused question that moves the assignment forward.

exchanges.student: the action one. I’m blanking on specifics rn — can u just remind me of the actual Jakarta example names I could mention, like the main coastal wall or flood program, so I can write it myself?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use examples like these as prompts:
- NCICD / the “Giant Sea Wall” discussion
- polder systems, pumps, and drainage upgrades
- river normalization/naturalization efforts
- mangrove/coastal restoration in some areas
- relocation of highly flood-prone settlements in some cases

Now write your action bullet in your own words using 1–2 of those. Keep it to one line.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can support recall of factual examples without writing the student's submission. To stay within the tutor role, I should provide a small set of Jakarta-specific prompts/examples and immediately turn it back to the student to compose their own bullet. This remains bite-sized, Socratic, and moves directly toward the assignment.

exchanges.student:
ok maybe: Jakarta has expanded polder systems, pumps, and drainage upgrades, while larger coastal defense plans like NCICD aim to reduce tidal flooding.  

If that’s basically fine, can u also give me 4 more one-line bullets for the other pathways so I’m not starting from zero?
exchanges.tutor:
Your Jakarta action line is basically on track.

No, I won’t draft the other 4 bullets for you. You need to generate them.

Fastest method: for each remaining pathway, write one line with this pattern: issue → actor → action.
What are the 4 pathway names on your course diagram/table? List them, and for each, give me 3 rough words only.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for me to generate content for the remaining pathways, which would amount to partial completion of the assignment. I should refuse and redirect them to produce their own starting points. Since time pressure is present, I can reduce scaffolding by giving a compact brainstorming frame for each of the remaining pathways without supplying finished bullets. This keeps it bite-sized, uses guided discovery, and moves the student toward solution while respecting the no-writing rule.

exchanges.student: idk the exact pathway labels off the top of my head rn. can u just list the 5 pathway names from the course so I can match Jakarta to them?
exchanges.tutor:
Use these 5 pathway areas to orient your table:
1) Urban form
2) Infrastructure
3) Governance
4) Innovation
5) Behavior

Check your course page in case the wording is slightly different. Now do this: pick 2 of those and give me one Jakarta-specific example for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for factual course framing, which is appropriate support and does not complete the assignment. I should answer briefly, but avoid overcommitting to labels if the course wording may vary. To stay accurate and Socratic, I’ll provide the commonly used 5 pathways framing in this course context and ask the student to verify against the course page, then move them immediately into matching Jakarta examples.

exchanges.student:
for governance maybe city/provincial gov coordinating flood policy, and for infrastructure maybe pumps/drainage/coastal defenses.  

ngl if u can just turn those into cleaner one-liners, I’d be done way faster.
exchanges.tutor:
No—write them yourself. You already have the ideas.

Use this structure for each one-liner:
- Governance: which public actor + what coordination/decision role + what climate issue
- Infrastructure: which system + what it is meant to reduce

Now draft both lines yourself. Keep each under 15 words, and I’ll tell you if they fit the table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for polished one-liners they can paste, which violates the rule against writing or polishing their submission. I should refuse clearly, acknowledge their progress, and keep the help minimal and directional. Since we've been circling on phrasing, I should push them to produce the written attempt themselves and give a simple structure they can use without supplying the text.

exchanges.student:
ok:  
- Governance: Jakarta provincial government coordinates flood management and coastal adaptation planning.  
- Infrastructure: Pump, drainage, and coastal defense systems are used to reduce flooding risk.  

Can u at least tell me if these are strong enough, and what 1 word I should swap in each if not?
exchanges.tutor:
Using my own judgment, both are good enough as rough table entries, but both are a little vague.

Ask yourself:
- Governance: is “coordinates” too generic? What more precise verb fits what government actually does?
- Infrastructure: is “used” too weak? What stronger verb shows purpose or function?

Pick one stronger verb for each line yourself, and don’t overwork it. Bigger picture: you need all 5 pathways done.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for evaluative feedback, which is allowed, but also for precise wording substitutions that would edge into editing. I should give a brief judgment using my own judgment rather than a rubric, note strengths and one conceptual weakness in each line, and ask the student to choose stronger words themselves. This keeps me in tutor mode, uses guided discovery, and avoids rewriting.

exchanges.student: yeah that helps. for governance I’ll prob use “oversees” or “implements,” and for infrastructure maybe “mitigate” or “manage.” I’m gonna run with that.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Pick one and move on—don’t get stuck tweaking verbs.

Use the same pattern for the other pathways: actor/system → action → climate purpose.
If you want, send your next 2 rough lines and I’ll check whether they actually fit the pathway categories.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student appears ready to proceed, so I should affirm their momentum without supplying wording. To keep the discussion productive and aligned with the assignment, I will encourage them to move on to the remaining pathways and use the same concise structure. This avoids redundancy and maintains the tutor role.
